elements.sent-2411-001: The Magellanic Stream has long been known to contain multiple H{ I strands and corresponding stellar populations are beginning to be discovered.}
elements.sent-2411-002: Combining an H3-selected sample with stars drawn from the {\it Gaia catalog, we trace stars along a sub-dominant strand of the Magellanic Stream, as defined by gas content, across 30$^\circ$ on the sky.}
elements.sent-2411-003: We find that the dominant strand is devoid of stars with Galactocentric distance $\lesssim 55$ kpc while the subdominant strand shows a close correspondence to such stars.
elements.sent-2411-004: We conclude that (1) the two Stream strands have different origins, (2) they are likely only close in projection, (3) the subdominant strand is tidal in origin, and (4) the subdominant strand is composed of disk material, likely drawn from the disk of the Small Magellanic Cloud.
elements.sent-2411-005: The Magellanic Clouds are the nearest example of a phenomenon that we understand to be common throughout the history of Universe --- the infall of small galaxies onto large dark matter halos \citep{blumenthal,davis .}
elements.sent-2411-006: As such, they present our best opportunity to study this central aspect of galaxy evolution and refine how it is modeled \citep[e.g.,][]{cole .}
elements.sent-2411-007: Is gravity or hydrodynamics primarily responsible for stripping gas out of such infalling galaxies \citep{mathewson,lin,moore ? How do galaxy interactions, either between the Clouds themselves or with the Milky Way (hereafter the MW), affect the star formation rates within the small galaxies \citep{z04,Harris_2009,massana ? How can such infall events affect the larger galaxy onto which the small galaxies have fallen \citep{weinberg,fox,gavarito,lucchini_21,carr ? To address these and other questions, investigators have generated an increasingly sophisticated set of numerical simulations of the interaction \citep[e.g.,][]{lin,gardiner,besla_10,diaz,gomez,pardy,gavarito,lucchini_21,lucchini_24,carr .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-008: This progress has led to a realization of the complexity of the system but also to a greater appreciation of its potential for leading us to a more complete understanding of such fundamental topics in astrophysics as the nature of the circumgalactic medium \citep{fox,lucchini_21,carr and dark matter \citep{foote .}}
elements.sent-2411-009: To motivate, constrain, and exploit even more complex simulations, an ever increasing set of detailed observational constraints is needed.
elements.sent-2411-010: Among the variety of interesting features of the Magellanic system that challenge models is the long gaseous tail that trails the Clouds, referred to as the Magellanic Stream \citep[hereafter the MS;][]{dieter,wannier,mathewson .}
elements.sent-2411-011: The MS is a set of apparently intertwined H{ I filaments \citep{cohen,morras,putman03,nidever whose origin (LMC, Bridge, or SMC) is still debated \citep[e.g.,][]{nidever_08,fox,diaz,pardy .}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: The 3D geometry is unknown because of the difficulty in measuring distances to gas clouds \citep{putman03 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: While arguments have often been framed around whether this gas was drawn out primarily by tidal forces \citep{fujimoto,lin or ram pressure \citep{meurer,moore , the unavoidable nature of tides, the weak, fragmented nature of the leading arm \citep{putman , the ionized nature of the MS \citep{putman_03a , and the morphology of high velocity clouds \citep{putman_11 suggest that both physical phenomena will be part of a full understanding.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Potential internal factors, such as winds that push gas outward and make it more susceptible to either tides or ram pressure, add yet another layer of complexity \citep{olano,nidever_08 .}
elements.sent-2411-015: The tidal hypothesis for the origin of the MS offers hope for the presence of stars along the MS, which would then provide distance constraints, a better understanding of the geometry and, perhaps, of its origin.
elements.sent-2411-016: In contrast, the confirmed absence of stars in the MS would likewise be an important constraint and would favor a hydrodynamic origin scenario, such as the blowout plus stripping model of \cite{nidever_08 .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Searches for stars along the MS have generally yielded negative results \citep{recillas,brueck,raja .}
elements.sent-2411-018: More recently, tidal stellar features have been uncovered closer to the LMC-SMC system \citep{belokurov16,mackey,belokurov,deason but a population tracing the MS has been elusive.}
elements.sent-2411-019: The H3 survey \citep{conroy and follow-up observations have contributed to the body of work on the stellar counterpart of the MS in two studies.}
elements.sent-2411-020: These, rather than the \citetalias{zar20 stars, appear to be more naturally identified as the stellar counterpart of the MS because of the angular momentum match and the closer agreement in distance with the simulations of \cite{besla12 .}}
elements.sent-2411-021: If the \citetalias{zar20 stars are not part of the MS, then what are they and what does the close association between these stars and at least some of the gas in the MS imply.}
elements.sent-2411-022: The \citetalias{zar20 detection involved only 15 stars, making it difficult to trace the feature in detail and establish an unambiguous association with any specific component of the Ms. Here we expand the sample by using the \citetalias{zar20 stars to aid us in selecting a {\it Gaia DR3 sample of stars with which to better trace this population.}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: In \S\ref{sec:data we describe how we select stars from the {\it Gaia catalog.}}
elements.sent-2411-024: In \S\ref{sec:results we describe the distribution of those stars and our inferences regarding the nature of the Ms..}
elements.sent-2411-025: Our strategy is to use the H3 catalog to identify a pure sample of putative stellar stream stars, as identified by \citetalias{zar20 , and then use those stars to train our selection of additional possible members of this population in the {\it Gaia catalog.}}
elements.sent-2411-026: We will then examine the resulting set of candidates to assess and interpret this population of stars and its association, if any, with the Ms..
elements.subsection-2411-001: H3 and the Selection of the Training Sample
elements.sent-2411-027: The H3 survey provides high-resolution spectroscopy of likely halo stars in a sparse grid covering roughly 15,000 square degrees \citep{conroy .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Likely halo stars are selected in high Galactic latitude fields ($\vert b \vert>30^{\circ}$ and Dec.
elements.sent-2411-029: For further details, see the H3 survey papers.
elements.sent-2411-030: We obtain spectra of as many of these stars as we can using the fiber-fed Hectochelle spectrograph \citep{hecto on the MMT in a configuration that produces spectra with a resolution of $\sim$ 32,000 from $5150$ to $5300$ \AA.}
elements.sent-2411-031: From these spectra, H3 catalogs the stellar parameters and spectro-photometric distances for $\approx$ 300,000 stars.
elements.sent-2411-032: The procedure we use in determining stellar parameters and distance estimates was developed and presented by \cite{cargile2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-033: The values of $V_{GSR}$ are quite precise given that $V_{RAD}$ is measured to $\sim$ 0.5 km sec$^{-1}$ precision \citep[based on repeat measurements;][]{conroy and the conversion to $V_{GSR}$ depends only on the position of the Sun in the Galaxy that we adopt.}
elements.sent-2411-034: From the available set of observed and analyzed H3 stars (rcat\_V4.0.5.d20240630\_MSG.fits) we select those that have no spectral fitting problems (${\rm FLAG = 0$), spectral signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) per pixel $>$ 2, are not identified as associated with the Sagittarius stream \citep[Sgr\_FLAG = 0;][]{johnson , are not identified as being part of a cold kinematic structure (satellite galaxies or stellar clusters; coldstr = 0) and have a Galactocentric distance $>$ 30 kpc.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: The Sgr cut corresponds to rejecting stars with $L_y < -2.5 - 0.3 L_z$, where the units are 10$^3$ kpc km s$^{-1}$\citep{johnson .}
elements.sent-2411-036: We use the \citetalias{zar20 results to help guide a slightly revised kinematic selection.}
elements.sent-2411-037: Originally, the selection was a straight cut on radial velocity.
elements.sent-2411-038: Here we select on total energy to help us find stars on nearly the same orbit across a range of distances.
elements.sent-2411-039: We adopt this approach in an attempt to detect stars both at smaller distances, where the crowding becomes more challenging, and at larger distances, where the radial velocity of any such stars might be significantly different than the original fixed cut.
elements.sent-2411-040: We set the energy threshold using the original set of stars and use galpy \citep{bovy to calculate the radial velocity as a function of distance in an NFW \citep{nfw potential corresponding to a Milky Way with total mass $\sim 10^{12 M_\odot$ \citep{z89,shen and then select stars within 30 km sec$^{-1}$ of that fiducial.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-041: As an acceptable simplification, the fiducial can be expressed as a third order polynomial: \begin{equation}\label{eq1} \begin{split} {\rm V}_{GSR} = 4.042\times 10^{-5} {\rm R}_{GAL}^3 - 0.0178 {\rm R}_{GAL}^2 + 2.87 {\rm R}_{GAL} - 295.1 \end{split}\end{equation} with R$_{GAL}$ expressed in kpc and V$_{GSR}$ in km s$^{-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-042: There is some arbitrariness in the selection of the 30 km sec$^{-1}$ tolerance but it is meant to represent the potential velocity dispersion of stars in the original unknown progenitor.
elements.sent-2411-043: Different choices of this value yield larger or smaller samples but do not qualitatively affect the results we present.
elements.sent-2411-044: Lastly, we limit the sample in the MS coordinate system \citep{nidever to have $-115^\circ < l_{MS < -65^\circ$ and $-20^\circ < b_{MS < 10^\circ$, which selects for stars in the vicinity of the Ms. Our selected set of stars is shown in Figures \ref{fig:selection and \ref{fig:all-sky .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-045: In Figure \ref{fig:selection , the H3 stars that lie within the shaded region but are not selected are those that do not satisfy our MS coordinate system criterion.}
elements.sent-2411-046: These stars are highlighted in blue in Figure \ref{fig:all-sky and appear to be a random subsample of halo stars that happen to satisfy the kinematic criteria but do not lie along the Ms. Note that in this context, the two stars near the tip of the MS are arguably part of the random halo sample rather than associated with the MS, but we retain them in our sample.}
elements.sent-2411-047: The sample of stars differs somewhat from the set of stars presented in \citetalias{zar20 , only 9 of 17 in the current set are in common.}
elements.sent-2411-048: This is primarily due to a change in Sgr\_FLAG that resulted in the rejection of 5 of the \citetalias{zar20 sample of 15.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Two of these five stars fall near the tip of the Ms. It is unclear if the addition of these stars helps to establish the population of stars associated with the MS tip or indicates that this population is better associated with Sgr.
elements.sent-2411-050: On the other hand, the remaining three stars fall well within the main group of stars and their inclusion or exclusion does not affect the interpretation of this population.
elements.sent-2411-051: We opt to continue to exclude the Sgr\_FLAG = 1 population but suspect that at least some of these stars may actually be part of the MS population we seek to identify.
elements.sent-2411-052: The one other missing star from \citetalias{zar20 now falls about 20 km sec$^{-1}$ outside our revised kinematic criteria.}
elements.sent-2411-053: Excluding these 6 stars does not affect our definition of the kinematic criterion.
elements.sent-2411-054: We will return to the topic of Sgr cross-contamination in \S\ref{sec:results .}
elements.sent-2411-055: The lack of H3 stars closer to the LMC and SMC along the MS is most likely due to the edge of the H3 survey footprint (see Figure \ref{fig:all-sky ).}
elements.sent-2411-056: The dearth of stars farther along the MS reflects either a lack of such stars in reality or only in the catalog.

elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.47\textwidth]{stream_selection.png} \caption{Selection of candidate H3 stream stars. All H3 stars with R$_{GAL} > 30$ kpc, FLAG = 0, SNR $> 2$, Sgr\_FLAG = 0, and coldstr = 0 are plotted as gray circles. Our kinematic selection criteria is represented by the shaded area and encompasses stars on orbits of similar total energy in a 10$^{12}$ M$_\odot$ NFW potential that represents the MW. The 17 stars satisfying this and the other selection criteria described in the text are shown as red circles and comprise our H3-selected sample. Distance uncertainties in H3 are estimated at $\sim 10$\% and radial velocity uncertainties are smaller than or comparable to the plotted symbols.} \label{fig:selection}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.47\textwidth]{stream_density.png} \caption{All-sky distribution in Galactic coordinates of the gaseous Magellanic Stream \citep{nidever} in green, the LMC and SMC in blue ellipses, the H3-selected stars that satisfy all of our criteria in red, and those that satisfy all of our criteria except for the one related to the MS coordinate system in blue. The footprint of the H3 survey, which lies outside the enclosed region and avoids the Galactic disk, precludes us from finding stars along the MS that are closer to the Clouds than those shown. } \label{fig:all-sky}
elements.sent-2411-059: Downselect Our approach will work only if the properties of the H3-selected stars, which have precise radial velocity and distance estimates, are sufficiently distinct within the parameter set measured by {\it Gaia that does not include these measurements to enable us to select corresponding stars.}
elements.sent-2411-060: Fortunately, our selected set of stars is both fairly consistent in its proper motion values and colors and distinct from the underlying population of stars (Figure \ref{fig:cuts ).}
elements.sent-2411-061: First, we apply the basic parallax cut imposed in H3 ($ \varpi < 0.4$ mas yr$^{-}$).

elements.sent-2411-063: We will trim more aggressively later but opt here to allow for some variation in proper motions among potential stream populations.

elements.sent-2411-066: The resulting population of 3,982 stars is presented in Figure \ref{fig:final_cuts showing both $\mu_{\alpha}$ and $\mu_{\delta}$ as a function of $l_{MS}$.}

elements.sent-2411-068: The selected stars can be divided into two sub-populations.
elements.sent-2411-069: The first is a set that matches that identified initially in H3.

elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=0.47\textwidth]{initial_cuts.png} \caption{The H3-selected stars (red circles) are localized relative to the full H3 sample in {\it Gaia} proper motion coordinates and color-magnitude space. Blue outlines mark our selection boxes.} \label{fig:cuts}
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=0.47\textwidth]{final_cuts.png} \caption{The distribution of proper motion values for stars selected using the criteria shown graphically in Figure \ref{fig:cuts} as function of MS longitude. For $\mu_\alpha$ and $\mu_\delta$ we show both the raw binned distribution and the smoothed distribution in blue. The H3-selected stars are superposed on the smoothed {\it Gaia} distributions as red circles. Green horizontal lines show our refined proper motion selection criteria. Stars between the two green lines are retained to produce Figure \ref{fig:mag_streams}.} \label{fig:final_cuts}
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{final_besla.png} \caption{Three views of a Magellanic Stream system. Top panel presents the HI distribution along the Magellanic Stream in Stream coordinates \citep{nidever}. Note the bifurcation of the Stream at $l_{MS} \sim -75^\circ$ with a small spur to lower $b_{MS}$ that plausibly extends, through a set of small clouds that we name the ``archipelago", out to $l_{MS}\sim -105^\circ$. The data are presented discretely and so the gas, despite presumably being spatially diffuse, is plotted as discrete points. Middle panel adds to this picture of the gaseous Stream the H3-selected stars (red circles) that appear to deviate from the principal Magellanic Stream and follow the spur. The curved line represents the edge of the H3 survey footprint. Bottom panel adds to this picture the {\it Gaia}-selected stars. The distribution of selected stars, smoothed as in Figure \ref{fig:final_cuts}, clearly follows the spur and perhaps, with less certainty, traces the HI archipelago all the way to $l_{MS} \sim -105^\circ$.} \label{fig:mag_streams}
elements.sent-2411-078: The populations of gas, H3-selected stars, and our final set of {\it Gaia -selected stars are presented in Figure \ref{fig:mag_streams .}}
elements.sent-2411-079: As noted previously, the MS is a set of apparently intertwined filaments \citep{cohen,morras,putman03,nidever .}
elements.sent-2411-080: Because we do not know the distances to the various filaments, the 3-D geometry, and therefore the origin of the structures, is unclear.
elements.sent-2411-081: Nevertheless, in the upper panel of the Figure there appear to be at least two clear strands of the MS over the values of $l_{MS}$ included in the Figure.
elements.sent-2411-082: The dominant strand appears to be one that extends from at least $l_{MS =-65^\circ$ to $l_{MS \sim -85^\circ$, after which there is a slight gap with the plausible continuation of gas extending from $l_{MS \sim -86^\circ$ to $l_{MS \sim -102^\circ$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-083: There is a second strand that bifurcates from the dominant strand at $l_{MS \sim -75^\circ$, forms a spur and dives to lower value of $b_{MS}$.}
elements.sent-2411-084: This strand plausibly continues along an ``archipelago" of H{ I clouds stretching out as far as the dominant stream but mostly at more negative values of $b_{MS}$ than the dominant Stream (i.e. at $b_{MS \sim -5^\circ$).}}
elements.sent-2411-085: We will refer to this strand as the subdominant one.

elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=0.9\columnwidth]{alpha.png} \caption{Chemical properties of H3-selected stars. We compare the properties of the H3 halo stars (gray), which satisfy all of our selection criteria except the kinematic and MS coordinate ones, to the H3-selected stars. elements.sent-2411-161: Combining a sample of 17 stars from the H3 (“Hectochelle in the Halo at High Resolution”) survey with 891 stars drawn from the {\it Gaia DR3 catalog, we trace stars along a sub-dominant strand of the MS, as defined by gas content, across 30$^\circ$ on the sky.}
elements.sent-2411-162: We find that the corresponding dominant strand at the similar position along the MS is devoid of stars with Galactocentric distance $\lesssim 55$ kpc while the subdominant strand shows a close correspondence to such stars.
elements.sent-2411-163: We conclude that (1) these two Stream strands have different origins, (2) they are likely only close in projection, (3) the subdominant strand is tidal in origin, and (4) the subdominant strand is composed of ``disk" material, gas and stars, with a chemical composition that marginally favors it coming from the Small Magellanic Cloud.
elements.sent-2411-164: The Magellanic Clouds are the nearest example of a phenomenon that we understand to be common throughout the history of the Universe --- the infall of small galaxies onto large dark matter halos \citep{blumenthal,davis .}
elements.sent-2411-165: Is gravity or hydrodynamics primarily responsible for stripping gas from such infalling galaxies \citep{mathewson,lin,moore ? How do galaxy interactions, either between the Clouds themselves or with the Milky Way (hereafter the MW), affect the star formation rates within the satellite galaxies \citep{z04,Harris_2009,massana ? How can such infall events affect the larger galaxy onto which the smaller galaxies have fallen \citep{weinberg,fox,gavarito,lucchini_21,carr ? To address these and other questions, investigators have generated an increasingly sophisticated set of numerical simulations of the interaction \citep[e.g.,][]{lin,gardiner,besla_10,diaz,gomez,hammer,pardy,gavarito,wang,lucchini_21,lucchini_24,carr,jimenez .}}}}

elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{final_besla.png} \caption{Three views of the Magellanic Stream system. The data are presented discretely and so the gas, despite presumably being spatially diffuse, is plotted as discrete points. Middle panel adds to this picture of the gaseous Stream the H3-selected stars (red circles) that appear to deviate from the principal Magellanic Stream and follow the spur. elements.sent-2411-253: Models of the MS show gas originating in both the LMC and SMC \citep{pardy .}
elements.figure-2411-015: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{chandra.png} \caption{Comparison of our set of stars to those identified by \citetalias{chandra}. The \citetalias{chandra} stars (green squares) are distributed over a far larger area of sky than our stars (red circles) and so only three or four overlap our search area. Those that are projected near our set of stars are at about a factor of 2 farther away and therefore likely trace a different physical structure. They were also selected to have different angular momenta. We plot the H{ \ I} distribution in the upper panel from \cite{nidever_08} and a curve showing the boundary of the H3 footprint for reference.} \label{fig:chandra}
elements.sent-2411-254: An alternative possibility is that the dominant strand is devoid of stars and arises either from tidally stripped gas that was originally at a radius beyond where there were many stars \citep{diaz or from gas that was blown out of the central galaxy and then stripped or tidally removed \citep{nidever_08 .}}
elements.sent-2411-255: Such stellar halo-like components have been traced in the periphery of the Clouds \citep{belokurov,ely,gaia-mc and likely have a complicated history \citep[e.g.,][]{massana24 .}}
elements.sent-2411-256: The subdominant MS strand investigated in the present work shows a close correspondence between stars and gas in the detailed density structure along the strand.
elements.sent-2411-257: The presence of stars confirms that this gaseous strand is a tidal feature.
elements.sent-2411-258: The metallicity and $\alpha$ enrichment of our H3-selected stars is consistent with an SMC origin.
elements.sent-2411-259: In support of such an interpretation, we note that absorption line studies of the MS \citep{fox13 and numerical simulations \citep{pardy suggest that much of the MS at these values of L$_{MS}$ originates from the SMC.}}
elements.sent-2411-260: Nevertheless, more complex scenarios cannot yet be definitively excluded.
elements.sent-2411-261: Forthcoming surveys --- chiefly the fifth-generation Sloan Digital Sky Survey from the southern hemisphere (SDSS-V; \citealt{Kollmeier2017 ) and 4MOST \citep{4most --- should be capable of resolving this question.}}
elements.sent-2411-262: Using an H3-selected sample of 17 stars that correspond to the population identified by \citetalias{zh3 as a potential stellar counterpart to the gaseous Magellanic Stream (MS), we selected 891 stars from the {\it Gaia catalog to expand the number of known stars in this component and to map this population.}}
elements.sent-2411-263: The dominant one, as defined by gas content, for $-65^\circ < L_{MS < -110^\circ$, is devoid of stars with Galactocentric distances $\lesssim 55$ kpc and may be traced by stars at larger distances (\citetalias{chandra ).}}
elements.sent-2411-264: Given the association between gas and stars, and the mean metallicity of the stars, we suggest that it is tidal material drawn from the body of the SMC, but this finding is marginally significant and requires additional confirmation.
elements.sent-2411-265: We thank MMTO staff and the CfA and U.
